    A: Cut surface of the brain with diffuse astrocytoma. B: Astrocytoma, H&E. C: Cut surface of the brain with glioblastoma multiforme. D: Microscopic GBM with hemorrhage, H&E. E: Gross surface of the cortex with metastatic melanoma. F: Cut surface of the brain with metastatic lung cancer.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-19
     
    A: Cut surface of the brain with diffuse astrocytoma. B: Astrocytoma, H&E. C: Cut surface of the brain with glioblastoma multiforme. D: Microscopic GBM with hemorrhage, H&E. E: Gross surface of the cortex with metastatic melanoma. F: Cut surface of the brain with metastatic lung cancer.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Evaluation of the CSF may help in the diagnosis. The CSF is usually clear, but it may occasionally reveal xanthochromia or be frankly bloody if there is hemorrhage into the tumor. The WBC count may be increased ≤150 cells/μL in up to 75% of patients and normal in others. Protein is usually increased. Protein is particularly increased with meningioma of the olfactory groove and with acoustic neuroma.
    •  
      Tumor cells may be demonstrable in up to 40% of patients with all types of solid tumors, but failure to find malignant cells does not exclude neoplasm. Atypical WBCs may be seen in leukemia or lymphoma. Tumor antigens/markers may indicate the source of some metastatic tumors. Glucose may be decreased if cells are present. OCBs may be present in patients with tumor and are nonspecific.
    •  
      Brain stem gliomas and “diencephalic syndrome,” which are characteristically found in childhood, are usually associated with normal CSF.

    Jugulotympanic Paraganglioma

    Definition

    This tumor arises from the jugular and tympanic parasympathetic ganglia within the ear and is the most common tumor of the middle ear. It is a slow-growing, vascular tumor, with blood supply from the external and/or internal carotid artery. It has the ability to secrete catecholamines. 

    Clinical Presentation

    The sporadic form is more common in women and the hereditary form is equally distributed in men and women. The tumor may result in hearing loss with pulsing/ringing in the ear, dizziness, and ear pain. Paragangliomas are a component of multiple endocrine neoplasia types 2A and 2B, neurofibromatosis type 1, von Hippel-Lindau, and Carney-Stratakis dyad. Nonsyndromic familial forms of paraganglioma are noted to have mutations in the SDH gene for which screening is available. 

    Laboratory Findings

    The diagnosis is made by neurophysiologic testing and CT or MRI. Blood and urine tests for endocrine workup (urinary and/or plasma fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines) should be performed. Evaluation of the CSF may reveal an increase in protein. Asymptomatic carriers of an SDH mutation may benefit from radiologic screening for early detection. 

    Leukemic Involvement of the Central Nervous System

    See Chapter 11, Hematologic Disorders. 

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Intracranial hemorrhage is the principal cause of death in leukemia (may be intracerebral, subarachnoid, or subdural). It is more frequent when the WBC count is >100,000/μL and with rapid increases in the WBC count, especially in blast crises. The platelet count is frequently decreased. Often, there is evidence of bleeding elsewhere. At autopsy, the tumor may be identified in the arachnoid, meninges, and perivascular regions (see eBook Figure 6-20).
       
       
      eFigure 6-20
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
       
      A: Low-power H&E section of meningeal leukemia, B: Perivascular cuffing of leukemia. C: High-power H&E section of acute leukemia.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      eFigure 6-20
       
      A: Low-power H&E section of meningeal leukemia, B: Perivascular cuffing of leukemia. C: High-power H&E section of acute leukemia.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    •  
      Evaluation of the CSF may be diagnostic. There may be intracranial hemorrhage and infiltration of leukemic cells (blasts) into the meninges and fluid. The CNS is involved in 5% of patients with ALL at diagnosis and is the major site of relapse. PCR is used to detect minimal residual cells that are not recognized morphologically. Involvement of the CSF by AML is less common than ALL and CLL, and plasmacytoid leukemias are very rare.
    •  
      The CSF may show an increased opening pressure and protein level. The glucose may be decreased to <50% of the blood level. Abnormal cells may be identified by cytochemical, immunohistochemical, immunofluorescent, or flow cytometric techniques to help diagnose leukemia.
    •  
      Evaluation of the CSF may also help in identifying complicating meningeal infection (e.g., various bacteria, opportunistic fungi).

    Lymphomatous Involvement of the Central Nervous System

    See Chapter 11, Hematologic Disorders. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Cytologic evaluation of the CSF may provide sufficient diagnostic material to avoid brain biopsy in some patients. The meninges are involved in <30% of patients with malignant lymphoma. Involvement is most prevalent in diffuse large cell (“histiocytic”), lymphoblastic, and immunoblastic lymphoma. CSF often reveals an elevated protein and a lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis. The glucose level is usually normal but may be decreased if there is leptomeningeal disease. Abnormal cells found in the CSF may be differentiated by immunohistochemistry, immunofluorescence, or flow cytometry. PCR immunoglobulin heavy chain rearrangement may also be used for identification of clonality. The demonstration of neoplastic lymphocytes in the CSF on cytologic evaluation or flow cytometry is sufficient for the diagnosis of CNS lymphoma. Stereotactic needle biopsy is performed if the CSF is nondiagnostic. 

    Spinal Cord Tumor

    Definition

    Spinal cord tumors occur within the cord parenchyma or membranes adjacent to the cord. They may be primary or metastatic. Primary spinal cord tumors account for 2–4% of all primary CNS tumors. Extradural tumors are usually metastatic and can cause spinal cord compression. Tumors arising within the dura, outside of the spinal cord, are termed intradural–extramedullary and comprise the nerve sheath tumors and meningiomas. Tumors arising within the spinal cord itself are called intramedullary tumors, predominantly gliomas (astrocytomas or ependymomas). 

    Clinical Presentation

    Patients present with progressive symptoms that vary based on the location of the compression of the cord and spinal nerve roots. Symptoms range from pain to loss of sensation or motor function and decreased sensitivity to heat or cold and bowel or bladder dysfunction. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Evaluation of the CSF reveals increased protein. The level may be very high and is associated with xanthochromia when there is a block of the subarachnoid space. The protein concentration is higher with complete block in cord tumors located at lower levels. Tumor cells may be demonstrable. Definitive diagnosis is made on tissue evaluation at biopsy (see eBook Figure 6-21). 
    eFigure 6-21
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Low-power H&E section of ependymoma. B: High-power view of tumor cords and rosettes.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-21
     
    A: Low-power H&E section of ependymoma. B: High-power view of tumor cords and rosettes.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Suggested Reading

    Duong LM, McCarthy BJ, McLendon RE, et al. Descriptive epidemiology of malignant and nonmalignant primary spinal cord, spinal meninges, and cauda equina tumors, United States, 2004–2007. Cancer. 2012;118(17):4220–4227.

    Congenital Disorders of the CNS

    Listen

    Neural Tube Defects

    Definition

    Neural tube defects (NTD) are due to a failure of the closure of the embryonic neural tube. They are second only to cardiac malformations as the cause of congenital anomalies. The supplementation of folic acid for all pregnant women has decreased the incidence of NTD markedly in the United States. NTD range from the most severe (anencephaly) to mild spinal defects (spina bifida) (see eBook Figure 6-22). 
    eFigure 6-22
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Meningocele in a premature infant. B: Meningomyelocele in a premature infant. C: Anencephaly. D and E: Total rachischisis (complete open neural tube).
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-22
     
    A: Meningocele in a premature infant. B: Meningomyelocele in a premature infant. C: Anencephaly. D and E: Total rachischisis (complete open neural tube).
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    The risk factors for NTD include folic acid deficiency, certain drugs (valproic acid, carbamazepine, and methotrexate), diabetes, obesity, and hyperthermia. Genetic factors may be associated with NTD, and fetuses with NTD have a high rate of karyotypic abnormalities. Trisomy 18 is the most common chromosomal abnormality found in NTD. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Screening for NTD should be offered to all pregnant women including high-quality second trimester ultrasound and/or alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) in the maternal serum. AFP screening should occur between 15 and 20 weeks, and results are expressed as multiples of the median (MoM) for each gestational week. Values >2.0–2.5 MoM are abnormal. AFP is more reliable for open NTD, and the detection rate for anencephaly is as high as 95%. Care should be taken in the interpretation of this test as it is affected by gestational age, maternal weight, maternal diabetes, multiple gestation, race, and fetal anomalies other than NTD. 

    Suggested Readings

    Driscoll DA, Gross SJ; Professional Practice Guidelines Committee. Screening for fetal aneuploidy and neural tube defects. Genet Med. 2009;11:818.
    Kennedy D, Chitayat D, Winsor EJ, et al. Prenatally diagnosed neural tube defects: ultrasound, chromosome, and autopsy or postnatal findings in 212 cases. Am J Med Genet. 1998;77:317.

    Mitochondrial Disorders

    •  
      Mitochondrial disorders are a clinically heterogeneous group of diseases caused by dysfunction of the mitochondrial respiratory chain resulting from mutations in mitochondrial (mtDNA) or nuclear-encoded genes (ntDNA) required for mitochondrial function.
    •  
      Mitochondrial DNA defects are transmitted by maternal inheritance. The human mtDNA is a double-stranded, circular molecule of 16 569 bp and contains 37 genes coding for two rRNAs, 22 tRNAs, and 13 polypeptides. The mtDNA-encoded polypeptides are all subunits of enzyme complexes of the oxidative phosphorylation system.
    •  
      The number of nuclear candidate genes responsible for mitochondrial disorders continues to grow. Many primary mitochondrial diseases caused by mutations in the mitochondrial genome involve multiple organ systems, reflecting the dependence on oxidative phosphorylation–derived energy in a wide variety of tissues. Heteroplasmy (copy number within a cell) and the varied distribution and dosage of mutant mitochondria in tissues and organs (mitotic segregation) also play a role in phenotypic presentation.
    •  
      The wide range of clinical presentations, frequent need for invasive testing procedures (e.g., muscle biopsy), and enormous genetic heterogeneity all contribute to the diagnostic challenge. There are “classic” clinical syndromes caused by mutations in the mitochondrial genes with distinguishing pathologic and muscle biopsy features such as Alpers disease (epilepsy and liver failure; POLG gene ntDNA mutations) and mtDNA disorders, MELAS (mitochondrial myopathy, encephalopathy, lactic acidosis and stroke-like episodes), MERRF (myoclonic epilepsy with ragged-red fibers), and NARP (neuropathy, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa). However, many patients exhibit only nonspecific features of DD or regression, further complicating arriving at accurate diagnoses.

    Suggested Readings

    Chinnery PF. Mitochondrial disorders overview. In: Adam MP, Ardinger HH, Pagon RA, et al., eds. Source GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle, WA: University of Washington, Seattle; 1993–2018. 2000 Jun 8 (updated 2014 Aug) MSeqDR: the Mitochondrial Disease Sequence Data Resource Consortium (https://mseqdr.org/).

    Trauma and Vascular Disorders of the CNS

    Listen

    Central Nervous System Trauma

    •  
      Laboratory findings vary depending on the type of brain injury (e.g., contusion, laceration, subdural hemorrhage, extradural hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage). There may also be laboratory findings due to complications of the brain injury (e.g., pneumonia, meningitis).
    •  
      Basilar skull fracture results in the disruption of the barriers between the sinonasal cavity and the anterior and middle cranial fossae and subsequent leak of CSF into the nasal cavity or ear (see eBook Figure 6-23). This communication with the CNS can lead to infectious complications, resulting in morbidity and mortality.
       
       
      eFigure 6-23
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      Basilar skull fracture.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      eFigure 6-23
      Basilar skull fracture.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Laboratory Evaluation for CSF Rhinorrhea

    Beta-2 transferrin is produced by neuraminidase activity within the CNS; it is found in the CSF, perilymph, and aqueous humor. Immunofixation electrophoresis with anti–transferrin-precipitating antibody is performed to differentiate CSF desialated transferrin from nasal secretions. The assay has a high sensitivity and specificity. 
    Beta-trace protein, also known as prostaglandin D synthase, has been used to diagnose CSF rhinorrhea in multiple studies, with a sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 100%. It is synthesized primarily in arachnoid cells, oligodendrocytes, and the choroid plexus, but it is also present in the testes, heart, and serum, and it is nonspecific for CSF. Prostaglandin D synthase may also be altered in renal failure, MS, cerebral infarction, and certain CNS tumors. 
    Testing for the glucose content in rhinorrhea with the use of glucose oxidase paper is not recommended for the following reasons: reducing substances in lacrimal gland secretions and nasal mucus may cause false-positive results, and meningitis may lower the glucose level in the CSF leading to a false-negative result. 

    Suggested Readings

    Michel O, Bamborschke S, Nekic M, Bachmann G. Beta-trace protein (prostaglandin D synthase) – a stable and reliable protein in perilymph. Ger Med Sci. 2005;3:Doc04.
    Ryall RG, Peacock MK, Simpson DA. Usefulness of beta 2-transferrin assay in the detection of cerebrospinal fluid leaks following head injury. J Neurosurg. 1992;77(5):737–739.

    Acute Epidural Hemorrhage

    Definition

    Epidural hemorrhage (bleeding between the dura and skull) is a rare but serious complication of head injury. It is most commonly due to an injury of the arteries predominantly the middle meningeal artery. It is found in up to 4% of patients surviving injury and up to 15% of autopsy series (see eBook Figure 6-24). 
    eFigure 6-24
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    Epidural hematoma.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-24
    Epidural hematoma.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      Patients may present with a lucid interval following the head injury. Expansion of the hematoma eventually causes pressure on the brain with shift of the tissues. Symptoms include abnormalities of cranial nerve III function or weakness of the extremities on the contralateral side. Severe bleeds may progress to transtentorial or uncal herniation and death.
    •  
      Epidural hemorrhage may also be due to nontraumatic etiologies including infection, coagulopathy, vascular and congenital malformations, and tumors. Rarely, it may occur during hemodialysis, pregnancy, and cardiac surgery. It may also occur in the epidural space of the spinal cord following spinal procedures.
    •  
      Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in cases where epidural hemorrhage is suspected, due to the risk of herniation. Diagnosis is primarily based on neuroimaging with unenhanced CT. Differentiation of epidural versus subdural hematoma can be made on imaging as epidural hematoma will not cross suture lines and has a lens-shaped appearance.

    Suggested Readings

    Bullock MR, Chesnut R, Ghajar J, et al. Surgical management of acute epidural hematomas. Neurosurgery. 2006;58:S7.
    Mayer S, Rowland L. Head injury. In: Rowland L, ed. Merritt's Neurology. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2000:401.

    Subdural Hematoma

    Acute

    Definition

    Acute subdural hematoma is most commonly due to the shearing effect on the bridging veins between the brain and the dural sinus during trauma. Occasionally, it may also be due to arterial injury or low CSF pressure. It is a complicating factor in up to 20% of severe traumatic brain injuries (see eBook Figure 6-25). 
    eFigure 6-25
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    Subdural hematoma.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-25
    Subdural hematoma.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    Most patients develop coma following injury, but some have a transient “lucid interval” after the acute injury, followed by a progressive neurologic decline to coma. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated due to the risk of herniation. The diagnosis is readily made on CT of the brain with subdural hematomas appearing as crescent-shaped lesions as opposed to the lens-shaped lesion of the epidural hematoma. 

    Chronic

    Definition

    Chronic subdural hematoma develops over days or weeks following minor injury that may not have been recognized by the patient. The bleed is slow and may be recurrent. 

    Clinical Presentation

    Chronic subdural hematoma is more commonly seen in the elderly and may present with the insidious onset of cognitive impairment, headaches, light-headedness, apathy, somnolence, or seizures. Symptoms may only appear weeks after the initial injury and may be transient or fluctuating. Diagnosis is based on CT imaging with membranous encapsulation visualized surrounding the hematoma. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated due to the risk of herniation. 

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      CSF is usually xanthochromic, and the CSF protein content is 300– 2,000 mg/dL.
    •  
      Anemia is often present in infants.

    Suggested Readings

    Bullock MR, Chesnut R, Ghajar J, et al. Surgical management of acute subdural hematomas. Neurosurgery. 2006;58:S16.
    Kaminski HJ, Hlavin ML, Likavec MJ, et al. Transient neurologic deficit caused by chronic subdural hematoma. Am J Med. 1992;92:698.
    Victor M, Ropper A. Craniocerebral trauma. In: Victor M, Ropper A, eds. Adams and Victor's Principles of Neurology, 7th ed. New York, NY: McGraw-Hill; 2001:925.

    Stroke

    Definition

    Stroke or cerebrovascular accident is the loss of function of an area of the brain due to vascular compromise. Stroke or ischemia of the brain may be transient or permanent. The causes include intracerebral (see eBook Figure 6-26) or subarachnoid hemorrhage (see eBook Figure 6-27) and thrombotic or embolic ischemia (see eBook Figure 6-28). The American Heart Association data review of strokes finds that the majority of strokes are due to ischemia (87%), followed by intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) (10%) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (3%). 
    eFigure 6-26
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    Intracerebral hemorrhage.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-26
    Intracerebral hemorrhage.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    eFigure 6-27
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Subarachnoid hemorrhage cortical surface. B: Subarachnoid hemorrhage basilar surface.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-27
     
    A: Subarachnoid hemorrhage cortical surface. B: Subarachnoid hemorrhage basilar surface.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    eFigure 6-28
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Acute thrombotic infarction with edematous shift of the midline seen on cross section of the brain. B: H&E section of acute infarct with intraparenchymal hemorrhage. C: H&E of old infarct with inflammation and liquefactive necrosis. D: Gross cut surface of the brain with resolving infarct. E: Basilar artery atherosclerosis. F: Cut section of basilar artery with atherosclerosis and thrombus.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-28
     
    A: Acute thrombotic infarction with edematous shift of the midline seen on cross section of the brain. B: H&E section of acute infarct with intraparenchymal hemorrhage. C: H&E of old infarct with inflammation and liquefactive necrosis. D: Gross cut surface of the brain with resolving infarct. E: Basilar artery atherosclerosis. F: Cut section of basilar artery with atherosclerosis and thrombus.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      The presenting signs depend on the size and location of the infarct. Risk factors include hypertension, trauma, drugs, smoking, alcoholism, atherosclerosis, and vascular malformations.
    •  
      The diagnosis of stroke is made on history, physical examination, and neurologic imaging (CT or MRI) to identify hemorrhagic versus thrombotic (or embolic) stroke and rule out mass lesion. If the blood pressure is normal, berry aneurysm, hemorrhage into tumor, angioma, or coagulopathy should be considered. Rapid diagnosis is important for the treatment of the patient as thrombolysis must be initiated within the first 4.5 hours after the initial event.
    •  
      In hemorrhagic stroke, the most common causes include ruptured berry aneurysm (45% of patients), hypertension (15% of patients), angiomatous malformations (8% of patients), and less commonly brain tumor and blood dyscrasia. Anticoagulant use is the most common cause of bleeding disorder resulting in stroke. Ischemic causes of stroke include thrombosis or embolism in 80% of patients and hypoperfusion.

    Laboratory Findings

    Blood testing at the time of suspected stroke should include CBC, PT, and PTT, thrombin time or ecarin clotting time (for patients taking a thrombin or factor Xa inhibitor), and lipid panel. A hypercoagulable panel including lupus anticoagulant (LA), anticardiolipin antibodies (ACAs), protein C, protein S, and factor V Leiden should also be obtained in cases of thrombotic stroke. Testing to rule out SLE may be indicated. Additional tests may include a fibrinogen level, ESR, serology for Lyme disease and HIV, and toxicology to rule out cocaine and other drugs. 

    Suggested Reading

    Benjamin EJ, Blaha MJ, Chiuve SE, et al. Heart disease and stroke statistics-2017 update: a report from the American Heart Association. Circulation. 2017;135:e146.

    Cerebral Embolism

    Definition

    •  
      Embolism is caused by particles of debris or tissue that originate distally (from vessel walls, the heart, or tumors) and travel through the circulation resulting in the blockage of arterial blood flow to the brain.
    •  
      Unlike thrombosis, which involves the local vessel, local therapy in embolic stroke is only temporizing. The source of the embolic fragment must be identified and treated, or additional events may occur.
    •  
      There are four categories of embolic stroke: those with known cardiac source, those with a possible cardiac or aortic source, those with an arterial source, and those with an unknown source (see eBook Figure 6-29). Cerebral embolism is the most common cause of stroke in the elderly.
       
       
      eFigure 6-29
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
       
      A: Posttraumatic fat emboli in the cortex and (B) cerebellum. C: Embolus in artery. D: Vascular embolus resulting in hemorrhage of the brain stem. E: Marantic vegetations of the cardiac valves is a common source of emboli.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      eFigure 6-29
       
      A: Posttraumatic fat emboli in the cortex and (B) cerebellum. C: Embolus in artery. D: Vascular embolus resulting in hemorrhage of the brain stem. E: Marantic vegetations of the cardiac valves is a common source of emboli.
       
      (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      Embolic stroke should be suspected if the change is sudden with maximal deficit at the onset, if the infarct is large, if there is a known cardiac or large arterial lesion, if the infarct is or becomes hemorrhagic on CT, if there are multiple lesions, and if clinical findings improve quickly.
    •  
      It is more common in patients with strokes of the posterior circulation. Atrial fibrillation, cardiac murmurs, and enlargement are risk factors for embolus of cardiac origin. A possible cardiac source should always be considered even in young patients. If the patient is febrile, septic emboli and endocarditis should be suspected. Small vessel (lacunar) stroke is most commonly seen in patients with hypertension, DM, or polycythemia. Patent foramen ovale is also a risk factor for venous to arterial embolism.
    •  
      The diagnosis of embolic stroke is primarily made on clinical examination and neuroimaging (MRI and CT scan). Cardiac evaluation with EKG and echocardiography is also helpful. Doppler studies of the large vessels of the neck and aorta may show lesions in these regions. Additional imaging studies to rule out myxoma of the left atrium, fat embolism in fracture of long bones, and air embolism following neck, chest, or cardiac surgery may be indicated.

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Laboratory tests should be performed to try to determine any underlying disorder and should include blood cultures to rule out bacterial endocarditis, hypercoagulable panel to rule out nonbacterial thrombotic vegetations on heart valves (LA, anticardiolipin, prothrombin mutation, factor V Leiden mutation), and cardiac enzymes to rule out underlying myocardial infarction with mural thrombus.
    •  
      Lumbar puncture with evaluation of the CSF may reveal findings similar to cerebral thrombosis. Hemorrhagic infarction develops in one third of patients post embolism, usually producing slight xanthochromia. Some patients may have grossly bloody CSF (10,000 RBCs/μL). Septic embolism (e.g., bacterial endocarditis) may cause increased WBC (CSF WBC count ≤200/μL with variable lymphocytes and PMNs), increased RBC (CSF RBC count ≤1,000/μL), slight xanthochromia, increased protein, normal glucose, and negative culture.

    Suggested Reading

    Jauch EC, Saver JL, Adams HP Jr, et al. Guidelines for the early management of patients with acute ischemic stroke: a guideline for healthcare professionals from the American Heart Association/American Stroke Association. Stroke. 2013;44:870.

    Intracerebral Hemorrhage

    Definition

    Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is defined as bleeding into the brain parenchyma and has many etiologies (see eBook Figure 6-30). 
    eFigure 6-30
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Intracerebral hemorrhage in an infant’s brain. B: Intracerebral hemorrhage in an adult brain consistent with hypertensive bleed in the watershed areas.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-30
     
    A: Intracerebral hemorrhage in an infant’s brain. B: Intracerebral hemorrhage in an adult brain consistent with hypertensive bleed in the watershed areas.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      ICH is the second most common cause of stroke following ischemic stroke. It is the cause of up to 15% of all first-time strokes, with a higher incidence in Asians, Hispanics, and African Americans. The risk for ICH is increased in patients with hypertension, amyloidosis, vascular malformations, and berry aneurysm. Risk also increases with age and alcohol and drug use. Etiologies of ICH include hypertensive vasculopathy, septic emboli, brain tumor, infection, vasculitis, bleeding disorders (including anticoagulants), and drugs such as cocaine and amphetamines.
    •  
      The diagnosis of intracranial hemorrhage is made by neurologic imaging (CT or MRI).

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Lumbar puncture reveals an increased CSF WBC count (15,000–20,000/μL), higher than in cerebral infarct (e.g., embolism, thrombosis). All patients should have a platelet count, PT, and PTT to establish bleeding potential. Tests that may be helpful include an elevated ESR in vasculitis and urinalysis, which may reveal transient glycosuria or concomitant renal disease.
    •  
      Additional tests may be obtained to rule out causes of ICH such as leukemia, aplastic anemia, polyarteritis nodosa, SLE, and other coagulopathies.

    Suggested Reading

    Flaherty ML, Woo D, Haverbusch M, et al. Racial variations in location and risk of intracerebral hemorrhage. Stroke. 2005;36:934.

    Berry Aneurysm (Saccular Aneurysm)

    Definition

    A berry or saccular aneurysm is a rounded dilation of an artery in the brain. The wall of the aneurysm is weaker than the normal vessel and therefore at risk for rupture with increased pressure (see eBook Figure 6-31). The majority of subarachnoid hemorrhages are due to ruptured saccular aneurysms. 
    eFigure 6-31
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Aneurysm of the basilar artery. B: Cross section of saccular aneurysm with compression of the brain stem.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-31
     
    A: Aneurysm of the basilar artery. B: Cross section of saccular aneurysm with compression of the brain stem.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      Patients with subarachnoid hemorrhage due to aneurysm rupture present with sudden severe headache, nausea, vomiting, vision loss, or loss of consciousness. There may be a sentinel headache following a minor bleed that precedes rupture.
    •  
      The incidence of saccular aneurysms is approximately 2% among the general population. The risk of rupture varies with aneurysm size, and most occur in the 40- to 60-year-old age group, with a slight increase in women over men. African Americans have a higher incidence than do Caucasians.
    •  
      Risk factors for saccular aneurysm include smoking, hypertension, genetic diseases (adult-dominant polycystic kidney disease, aldosteronism, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome), family history, sympathomimetic drugs such as phenylpropanolamine and cocaine, and decreased estrogen as is seen in postmenopausal women.
    •  
      The diagnosis of saccular aneurysm rupture is made by identification of symptomatology and laboratory tests. CT scan will identify subarachnoid clot.

    Laboratory Findings

    Lumbar puncture reveals increased opening pressure and elevated RBC count that does not decrease between tubes 1 and 4. Bloody CSF clears by the 10th day in 40% of patients. On occasion, hemorrhage is wholly within the parenchyma, resulting in normal CSF findings. 

    Suggested Readings

    Beetham R; UK NEQAS for Immunochemistry Working Group. Recommendations for CSF analysis in subarachnoid haemorrhage. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 2004;75:528.
    de Rooij NK, Linn FH, van der Plas JA, et al. Incidence of subarachnoid haemorrhage: a systematic review with emphasis on region, age, gender, and time trends. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 2007;78:1365.
    Vernooij MW, Ikram MA, Tanghe HL, et al. Incidental findings on brain MRI in the general population. N Engl J Med. 2007;357:1821.

    Cerebral Vein or Sinus Thrombosis

    Definition

    Cerebral venous and dural sinus thrombosis is the presence of clot within the sinus. 

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      Cerebral vein and dural sinus thromboses are an uncommon cause of stroke. They are more likely to occur in neonates and children than adults and more likely in women than in men.
    •  
      The causes of thrombosis include prothrombotic conditions in 85% of cases (oral contraception, pregnancy, malignancy), infection (e.g., otitis, mastoiditis, sinusitis, meningitis), and head injury.
    •  
      Genetic disorders may also be implicated, including antithrombin III deficiency, protein C and protein S deficiency, factor V Leiden mutation, and prothrombin gene mutation. Collagen vascular and inflammatory diseases (e.g., SLE, sarcoidosis, and Wegener granulomatosis), vasculitis (e.g., polyarteritis nodosa, Takayasu syndrome, dissecting aneurysm of the aorta, syphilis, meningitis; see Chapter 5, Cardiovascular Disorders), and hypotension (e.g., myocardial infarction, shock) are other potential causes of cerebral vein thrombosis.
    •  
      Venous thrombosis results in elevated venous pressure followed by leakage into the surrounding parenchyma and resultant impairment of CSF reabsorption leading to increased intracranial pressure. Patients may present with headache that increases over several days, motor weakness, paresis, or seizures. Thromboses of the cerebral veins and sinuses may produce focal hemorrhagic infarcts, which can be seen on neuroimaging (see eBook Figure 6-32).
       
       
      eFigure 6-32
      Full Size  |  Slide (.ppt)
      FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
      Hemorrhage secondary to venous thrombosis, cut surface of the brain.
       
      (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      eFigure 6-32
      Hemorrhage secondary to venous thrombosis, cut surface of the brain.
       
      (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
      View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
       | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    •  
      The diagnosis of cerebral vein thrombosis is made predominantly by neurologic imaging (CT or MRI).

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Laboratory tests are nonspecific but may give clues to the etiology. Routine hematology, coagulation (PT, PTT), and chemistry tests are recommended and give an idea of underlying cause. Evaluation of an underlying hypercoagulable state with antithrombin, proteins C and S, factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, LA, anticardiolipin, and anti-β2 glycoproteins may be helpful. An elevated d-dimer may support the diagnosis but will not exclude it if negative.
    •  
      Lumbar puncture for evaluation of CSF to rule out infection is required. Up to 50% of patients may have CSF findings. The CSF may reveal normal or mildly increased protein to ≤100 mg/dL. The CSF cell count may be normal or ≥10 WBC/μL during the first 48 hours and rarely ≥2,000 WBC/μL transiently on the 3rd day. Red cells may be increased.
    •  
      Additional blood tests may be informative. An increased CRP and ESR are risk factors for development of stroke, and an increased CRP is associated with a poorer short-term prognosis. Hematologic disorders (e.g., polycythemia, sickle cell disease, thrombotic thrombocytopenia, and macroglobulinemia) may be identified (see Chapter 11, Hematologic Disorders).

    Suggested Readings

    Saposnik G, Barrinagarrementeria F, Brown RD Jr, et al. Diagnosis and management of cerebra venous thrombosis: a statement for healthcare professionals from the American Heart Association/American Stroke Association. Stroke. 2011;42:1158.
    Stam J. Thrombosis of the cerebral veins and sinuses. N Engl J Med. 2005;352:1791.

    Hypertensive Encephalopathy

    Definition

    Hypertensive encephalopathy is usually associated with a blood pressure of ≥180/120 mm Hg and is an acute, life-threatening disorder presenting with signs of cerebral edema (see eBook Figure 6-33). 
    eFigure 6-33
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    Cut surface of the cortex with acute and resolving foci of hemorrhage due to hypertensive encephalopathy.
     
    (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-33
    Cut surface of the cortex with acute and resolving foci of hemorrhage due to hypertensive encephalopathy.
     
    (Image from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    Clinical symptoms are characterized by the insidious onset of headache, nausea, and vomiting. If not treated, patients progress to mental confusion, seizures, and coma. Although these symptoms differ from the abrupt onset of stroke, an MRI scan should be obtained, which may reveal edema in the parieto-occipital regions (reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy) or in the pontine region (hypertensive brain stem encephalopathy). 

    Laboratory Findings

    Findings on laboratory tests are due to changes in other organ systems and to underlying conditions such as cardiac, renal, and endocrine disorders and toxemia of pregnancy. Testing may also reveal changes that may occur due to progressive disorders following hypertensive encephalopathy such as focal ICH. The CSF frequently shows increased pressure and protein ≤100 mg/dL. 

    Suggested Reading

    Kitaguchi H, Tomimoto H, Miki Y, et al. A brain stem variant of reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome. Neuroradiology. 2005;47:652.

    Thrombophlebitis of Cavernous Sinus

    Definition

    Thrombophlebitis or inflammation of the vein results from infected or septic thrombosis of the venous sinus. 

    Clinical Presentation

    Septic dural sinus thrombosis has become a rare disease since the advent of antibiotics. Patients present with headache, occasionally eye swelling or diplopia, and alterations in mental status. The diagnosis is primarily made by imaging studies; however, lumbar puncture can be supportive, differentiating periorbital cellulitis from septic cavernous sinus thrombosis. 

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Laboratory findings that may be helpful include the CBC where an elevated peripheral WBC count may suggest an acute bacterial infection or other causes of venous thromboses such as sickle cell disease, polycythemia, or dehydration. The CSF reveals inflammatory cells in 75% of cases with increased neutrophils or mononuclear cells, elevated protein, normal glucose, and a negative culture. Thirty percent of patients have a CSF finding consistent with bacterial meningitis with a positive culture.
    •  
      Culture of the CSF reveals Staphylococcus aureus in 70% of cases and is associated with facial infection or sphenoid sinusitis. MRSA is becoming more frequent. Streptococci are less commonly identified. Anaerobes may be seen in association with sinus, dental, or tonsillar infections. Fungal pathogens have also been reported.

    Suggested Readings

    Naesens R, Ronsyn M, Druwé P, et al. Central nervous system invasion by community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. J Med Microbiol. 2009;58:1247.
    Southwick FS, Richardson EP Jr, Swartz MN. Septic thrombosis of the dural venous sinuses. Medicine (Baltimore). 1986;65:82.

    Spinal Cord Infarction

    Definition

    Spinal cord infarction may result from occlusion of the anterior spinal artery and the posterior spinal artery, venous occlusion, or hypoperfusion. 

    Clinical Presentation

    Symptoms of paresis either unilateral or bilateral occur with infarction of the anterior spinal artery and loss of touch, proprioception, and vibratory sense with infarction of the posterior spinal artery. Venous infarction may also occur and is usually associated with vascular malformations. The differential diagnosis includes transverse myelitis, compression, and acute polyneuropathy. Diagnosis is made primarily by neuroimaging (MRI) and vascular imaging (CTA or MRA). 

    Laboratory Findings

    Lumbar puncture is recommended in younger patients to rule out infectious or inflammatory etiologies including cell count, glucose, protein, gram stain, and culture. In spinal infarct, the CSF may be normal or may show mild pleocytosis with WBC < 100 and mild elevation in protein. Infectious agents such as Lyme, herpes, varicella, coxsackie, EBV, syphilis, and CMV should be ruled out with serology. Blood and urine toxicology should be performed to rule out cocaine. Additional blood tests to rule out hypercoagulable state and collagen vascular disease may be of benefit. 

    Suggested Reading

    Novy J, Carruzzo A, Maeder P, Bogousslavsky J. Spinal cord ischemia: clinical and imaging patterns, pathogenesis, and outcomes in 27 patients. Arch Neurol. 2006;63:1113.

    CNS Vasculitis

    Definition

    Vasculitis is an inflammation of the vessels that may occur within the CNS, and most commonly it is due to collagen vascular disease but may also be due to infection, atherosclerosis, embolic disease, malignancy, and drugs. Primary angiitis of the CNS is a rare disorder of unknown etiology presenting predominantly in men and may occur at any age (see eBook Figure 6-34). Giant cell arteritis is one of the more common forms of vasculitis. 
    eFigure 6-34
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    View OriginalDownload Slide (.ppt)
    FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
     
    A: Gross view of necrotizing vasculitis of the basilar artery. B: Cut surface os brain showing punctate hemorrhage and edema from necrotizing vasculitis. C: Cross section of vessel with marked inflammation and thrombus, H&E. D: High-power H&E showing inflammation in the arterial wall.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    eFigure 6-34
     
    A: Gross view of necrotizing vasculitis of the basilar artery. B: Cut surface os brain showing punctate hemorrhage and edema from necrotizing vasculitis. C: Cross section of vessel with marked inflammation and thrombus, H&E. D: High-power H&E showing inflammation in the arterial wall.
     
    (Images from Juliana Szakacs, MD, MSW.)
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites
    View Original | Download Slide (.ppt)
     | FavoriteAdd To Remove From Your Favorites

    Clinical Presentation

    Patients may present with various neurologic symptoms depending on the area of the brain affected and the severity of the disease. In giant cell arteritis, patients present with neurologic symptoms of headache and visual disturbances. Amyloid angiopathy (see Vascular Dementia) may also occur in the CNS. The differential diagnosis of vasculitis is broad, and diagnostic testing includes clinical examination and history, neuroimaging such as MRA or CTA, and evaluation of the blood and CSF. 

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Blood work should include a sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, which may be elevated in collagen vascular diseases, infection, and temporal arteritis. These are normal in primary angiitis. Serologic evaluation should be carried out to rule out syphilis, Lyme disease, cat scratch disease, tuberculosis, herpes virus, hepatitis B and C virus, HIV, and cysticercosis. Rheumatologic studies should be performed to rule out collagen vascular disease (ANA, RF, antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody).
    •  
      A lumbar puncture with analysis of the CSF should be performed to rule out infection (cell count and culture), malignancy (cytology), or hemorrhage with evidence of xanthochromia.
    •  
      If the diagnosis cannot be made with less invasive means, a biopsy should be performed. Biopsy will help in the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis and primary angiitis of the CNS and exclude malignancy or infection. Vascular changes include loss of the internal elastic lamina and inflammatory infiltrates of the vascular wall by histiocytes, giant cells, and lymphocytes.

    Suggested Reading

    Salvarani C, Brown RD Jr, Calamia KT, et al. Primary central nervous system vasculitis: analysis of 101 patients. Ann Neurol. 2007;62:442.

    Paraneoplastic Syndromes Affecting the CNS

    Listen

    Definition

    The paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes are a group of disorders resulting from an immunologic response directed against a normal antigen of the nervous system. These may be shared antigens with a tumor resulting in classification of the antigen as foreign by the immune system. Antibodies to intracellular neuronal proteins comprise the classical paraneoplastic antibodies such as anti-Hu, which are almost always associated with a tumor and are markers for a cytotoxic T-cell–mediated mechanism of disease. Antibodies against cell surface or synaptic proteins such as anti GABA-A receptor may occur with or without a tumor and are directly cytotoxic. 

    Clinical Presentation

    Patients present with muscle weakness and autonomic dysfunction as part of one of a number of paraneoplastic syndromes: encephalomyelitis, limbic encephalitis, subacute cerebellar degeneration, LEMS, chronic gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction, opsoclonus–myoclonus, subacute sensory neuronopathy, and dermatomyositis. 
    Antibodies can be detected in both the serum and CSF of patients with these disorders. Patients with small cell lung carcinoma may have multiple antibodies with or without symptoms. Some patients without an underlying carcinoma may also have antibodies causing neurologic disease. Paraneoplastic symptoms and antibodies may be present years before a malignancy is diagnosed. The identification of antibodies in a patient does not necessarily predict symptomatology. 

    Laboratory Findings

    In patients suspected of a classical paraneoplastic syndrome, serum will reveal the antibody. The well-characterized paraneoplastic antibodies and associated tumors include anti-Hu (small cell lung cancer [SCLC]), anti-Yo (GYN, breast), anti-Ri (SCLC, GYN, breast), anti-Tr (Hodgkin lymphoma), anti-CV2/CRMP5 (SCLC, thymoma), anti-Ma 1 and 2 (germ cell, lung, other), antiamphiphysin (breast, lung), anti-VGCC (SCLC), anti-PCA-2 (SCLC), antibipolar cells of the retina (melanoma), and antirecoverin (SCLC). 
    In addition to evaluation of antibodies in the CSF, spinal fluid should be evaluated for malignant cells by cytology and for inflammatory changes such as pleocytosis and OCBs to rule out MS. 

    Suggested Readings

    Dalmau J, Geis C, Graus F. Autoantibodies to synaptic receptors and neuronal cell surface proteins in autoimmune diseases of the central nervous system. Physiol Rev. 2017;97:839.
    Graus F, Saiz A, Dalmau J. Antibodies and neuronal autoimmune disorders of the CNS. J Neurol. 2010;257:509.

    Autoimmune Dementia

    Autoimmune dementia is a rapidly progressive dementia that is responsive to steroids. It is more common in females. The etiology is unknown. 

    Immune-Mediated Seizures

    There are several types of immune-mediated encephalitis in children that may present with seizures. Children usually do not have an underlying neoplasm. The encephalitis may be associated with anti-NMDAR or other synaptic proteins such as Caspr2. Although this is a rare disorder, it is responsive to immunotherapy and therefore important to recognize. 

    Suggested Reading

    Suleiman J, Wright S, Gill D, et al. Autoantibodies to neuronal antigens in children with new-onset seizures classified according to the revised ILAE organization of seizures and epilepsies. Epilepsia. 2013;54:2091.

    Myasthenia Gravis

    Definition

    Myasthenia gravis is a disorder of neuromuscular transmission resulting from the development of autoantibodies directed against the acetylcholine receptor (AChR) or muscle-specific tyrosine kinase (MuSK). This results in the destruction of proteins in the postsynaptic membrane of the neuromuscular junction. 

    Clinical Presentation

    The majority of patients presenting with myasthenia gravis have thymoma and occasionally SCLC, thyroid, or breast cancer. Patients present with fluctuating weakness of the extraocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles. Weakness of the eyelid and extraocular muscles is present in most patients and precedes limb weakness; limb weakness alone is rare in myasthenia gravis. The differential diagnosis includes the LEMS, thyroid ophthalmopathy, ALS, botulism, and cranial nerve or brain stem pathology. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Diagnosis is made by physical examination (Tensilon or ice pack test), history, and serologic testing for AChR or MuSK antibodies. These tests are confirmatory in up to 85% of patients with generalized disease. The most sensitive test for AChR-ab is the binding antibody test by radioimmunoassay, which is highly specific for myasthenia gravis. Antibody titers do not correlate with severity of disease and correlate poorly between patients. MuSK antibodies may be detected in up to half of the patients who are negative for AChR antibodies and may correlate with disease severity. 

    Suggested Reading

    Meriggioli MN, Sanders DB. Muscle autoantibodies in myasthenia gravis: beyond diagnosis? Expert Rev Clin Immunol. 2012;8(5):427–438.

    Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome

    Definition

    LEMS is an autoimmune disease associated with malignancy and results from the development of antibodies to the voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC), which interfere with the normal calcium flux required for the release of acetylcholine. It is strongly associated with SCLC but occurs less frequently than myasthenia gravis. 

    Clinical Presentation

    Patients present with symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, which starts in the lower extremities (difficulty rising from a chair), and autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth). The differential diagnosis includes myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, polyneuropathies, and multiple cranial mononeuropathies. The diagnosis is made on clinical examination and confirmed by electrodiagnostic studies and the presence of antibodies to P/Q type VGCC in the serum. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Testing for VGCC is by radioimmunoassay. Two separate antibodies can be identified. The P/Q-type VGCC is found in 85–95% of patients and all patients with a diagnosis of cancer. The N-type VGCC is found in approximately 40% of patients with LEMS and is more likely to be seen in patients with small cell lung carcinoma. Higher titers of antibody are found in patients with underlying carcinoma, while lower titers of antibody may be seen in patients with other neurologic paraneoplastic disorders and ALS. 

    Suggested Readings

    Lennon VA, Kryzer TJ, Griesmann GE, et al. Calcium-channel antibodies in the Lambert-Eaton syndrome and other paraneoplastic syndromes. N Engl J Med. 1995;332:1467.
    Pellkofer HL, Armbruster L, Krumbholz M, et al. Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome differential reactivity of tumor versus non-tumor patients to subunits of the voltage-gated calcium channel. J Neuroimmunol. 2008;204:136.

    Infections of the Central Nervous System

    Listen
    Infections of the CNS are associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Infections may be caused by all types of pathogens from viruses to parasites. Organisms may gain access to CNS most commonly by hematogenous spread (e.g., endocarditis), direct extension from a contiguous site of infection (e.g., infected sinus), or direct inoculation (e.g., surgery, trauma, basilar skull fracture). 
    Pathogenesis and signs and symptoms depend on the pathogen, site of infection, and coexisting conditions. Infection may occur in the brain parenchyma, as seen in encephalitis and brain abscess, or outside the parenchyma in locations bounded by the meninges. Meningitis occurs in the subarachnoid space (between the arachnoid and pia mater). Epidural abscesses are localized in the space between the dura mater and the vertebrae. Subdural abscesses are localized in the space between the dura mater and arachnoid. 

    Central Nervous System Abscesses

    •  
      * Most CNS abscesses are localized bacterial infections with formation of pus. A wide variety of bacterial pathogens have been implicated; the etiology depends primarily on the site of primary infection or source of organisms. Monomicrobial and polymicrobial infections are well defined.
    •  
      In immunocompromised patients, fungal and parasitic pathogens should be considered. Toxoplasma gondii reactivation may occur in patients with defects in cell-mediated immunity, like HIV infection. Other parasitic pathogens, like Taenia solium or Entamoeba histolytica, must be considered in patients who have emigrated from endemic areas.
    •  
      Disease symptoms are caused by the inflammatory and toxic effects of the infection as well as mechanical forces of a growing mass within the rigid structure of the skull or spinal column.

    Who Should Be Suspected?

    •  
      Risk factors for CNS abscess include bacteremia (especially staphylococcal or streptococcal), trauma, recent neurosurgical or neurodiagnostic procedure, immunocompromising medical conditions, and infections in adjacent bone or soft tissue sites (e.g., sinusitis, otitis media, dental abscess, vertebral diskitis, osteomyelitis).
    •  
      Patients with arteriovenous malformations or other right-to-left shunts are at significantly increased risk for brain abscesses.

    Clinical Presentation

    •  
      The clinical presentation may be complicated by signs and symptoms of the underlying conditions or primary sites of infection, like bacteremia, severe sinusitis, or diskitis, which lead to the CNS infection.
    •  
      Abscesses of the brain parenchyma: Severe headache, usually localized to the side of the abscess, is the most common and earliest symptom (~70%). Vomiting may occur because of increased intracranial pressure, and cerebral edema may cause mental status changes. Late manifestations may include focal neurologic deficits, including cranial nerves, papilledema, and seizures.
    •  
      Intracranial epidural abscesses: In most patients, there is a history of a neurologic procedure or pathology of the skull overlying the dura, and these may initially cause the symptoms of primary concern. Patients initially complain of severe headache. Signs and symptoms associated with infection and increasing intracranial pressure, as described for brain abscess, may develop as the infection expands.
    •  
      Spinal epidural abscesses: The classic triad of fever, spinal pain, and neurologic deficits may develop only slowly after initial symptoms. Fever may be absent, but is an important clue when present. Focal back pain increases in severity and progress to neurologic symptoms. These may include pain in the distribution of the affected nerve root, followed by functional deficits, including weakness/paralysis, sensory changes, and organ dysfunction.

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      Early suspicion is critical for timely diagnosis of CNS abscesses. Imaging studies identify the location and characteristics of the infected tissues. Because of the broad etiology of agents, it is then critical to obtain material for culture by aspiration of the abscess.
    •  
      Aspirate of infected pus should be cultured for aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, fungi, and mycobacteria, with Gram, AFB, and fungal stains. See Chapter 3 for test details and transport requirements.
    •  
      Histopathologic examination may provide specific diagnosis.
    •  
      Blood cultures are positive in approximately 10% of patients. Multiple brain abscesses are most likely due to hematogenous seeding.
    •  
      CSF may provide diagnostic information, but may not be collected because of concerns for increased intracranial pressure. In localized parenchymal, epidural, and subdural abscesses, organisms may not have access to the CSF, so Gram stain and culture of CSF are often negative. On the other hand, the immune response to abscesses may result in inflammatory changes detectable in the CSF, like increased WBC (usually without clear PMN predominance) and mildly elevated protein; CSF glucose is typically normal.
    •  
      Toxoplasma serology is recommended in patients with HIV infection. Other specific serologic testing is performed on the basis of epidemiologic risk.

    Encephalitis, Viral

    •  
      Encephalitis is a disease characterized by diffuse or localized inflammation of the brain parenchyma associated with neurologic dysfunction. Historically, viruses have been primary in the infectious etiology of encephalitis. Effective vaccination has reduced the incidence of several of the viruses that have been prominent causes of encephalitis, like mumps and measles viruses.
    •  
      The range of pathogens capable of causing encephalitis is broad. A specific diagnosis cannot be established in a significant number of patients with suspected infectious encephalitis. In patients in whom a diagnosis is established, approximately 50% are viral.
    •  
      A number of viruses are capable of causing encephalitis, either by direct infection or as an immune-mediated postinfectious syndrome. Influenza; measles, mumps, and rubella; and varicella–zoster viruses (VZV) have all been implicated in postinfectious encephalitis. HSV, usually type 1, causes up to 75% of cases of diagnosed viral encephalitis. VZV, enteroviruses, and arboviruses, including West Nile virus, account for most other diagnoses.

    Who Should Be Suspected?

    Patients present with headache, nausea, and vomiting; fever may be present. Patients usually develop changes in mental status, from subtle behavioral changes to frank obtundation. Seizures are common. Focal neurologic abnormalities may occur. Nuchal rigidity suggests a meningeal component (meningoencephalitis or isolated meningitis). Risk factors for viral encephalitis are poorly understood. 

    Laboratory Findings

    Detailed history and physical examination are important in the assessment of patients, including season, travel history, animal or unusual contacts, etc. Consultation with local departments of health may provide insight related to circulating viruses. Temporal lobe involvement may suggest HSV infection. Preceding flaccid paralysis is suggestive of West Nile virus infection. Specific diagnostic testing should prioritize agents with the highest pretest probability based on presenting signs, symptoms, and epidemiology: 
    •  
      Imaging studies: MRI abnormalities provide important information related to the diagnosis and possibly the likely virus.
    •  Specific diagnostic testing: (See Chapter 3 for details of specific tests) Nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) of CSF provide the most reliable information. Though multiplex panels are available for CNS pathogens, targeted, single-agent NAAT testing is usually used based on pretest probability assessment.  
      •  
        First-line NAAT targets: HSV-1 and HSV-2, VZV, and enteroviruses (including human parechovirus in young children). Consider nasopharyngeal and stool RT-PCR for enterovirus diagnosis.
      •  
        First-line serology: CSF IgM for VZV and WNV/arboviruses and serum IgG and IgM for WNV/arboviruses.
      •  
        Additional first-line tests for immunocompromised patients: CSF NAAT for CMV, HHV-6, HHV-7, JC virus, and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV). CSF serology for LCMV. Serum CMV viral load. Serum serology: HHV-6, HHV-7, JC virus, and LCMV.
      •  
        Second-line targets should be pursued if first-line testing does not provide a diagnosis or if specific clinical or epidemiologic evidence suggests another agent. Suspect adenovirus, EBV, HIV, HHV-6, HHV-7, measles, mumps, and parvovirus B19. Diagnose using specific NAAT and serologic testing of CSF and serum.
    •  Other testing:  
      •  
        CSF usually shows signs of inflammation, but these may be nonspecific. Findings overlap with aseptic meningitis and paraspinal abscesses. There is usually mild to moderate CSF pleocytosis (<250 cells/mm3), with lymphocyte predominance. The presence of significant numbers of RBCs suggests a necrotizing encephalitis, like HSV. Protein may be mildly elevated (<150 mg/dL). CSF glucose is usually not decreased (>50% of simultaneous serum glucose concentration).
      •  
        CSF viral culture has a low diagnostic yield for CNS infections, especially for nonenteroviral and non-HSV CNS infections.
      •  
        Brain biopsy, with routine and immunohistologic staining, may provide specific diagnosis for patients in whom initial testing by noninvasive testing is negative. In patients with postinfectious encephalitis, the virus responsible for the inflammatory response cannot be isolated from affected tissue.

    Meningitis

    •  
      Meningitis generally refers to infection in the subarachnoid space, the space between the middle (arachnoid) layer and the layer adjacent to the neural tissue (pia mater).
    •  
      In acute meningitis, symptoms develop within several days, often within hours. Common community-acquired bacterial pathogens include Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Listeria monocytogenes. In infants, group B Streptococcus is a significant cause of meningitis. Staphylococci and aerobic gram-negative pathogens are associated with traumatic and postsurgical meningitis. Enteroviruses cause approximately 90% of cases of aseptic meningitis. Other neurotropic viruses that cause encephalitis may also cause meningitis. Nonpyogenic bacterial and fungal pathogens, including Cryptococcus neoformans, mycobacteria, and parasites, should be considered in immunocompromised patients, patients with infections at other sites, or patients with specific epidemiologic exposures or risk factors.

    Who Should Be Suspected?

    •  
      Bacteria that gain access to the subarachnoid space are able to proliferate efficiently, resulting in the rapid onset of sepsis and severe signs and symptoms. A significant proportion of adult patients with community-acquired acute bacterial meningitis (ABM) do not present with all of the classic clinical features (headache, fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status), but the majority will show at least two of the four.
    •  
      A significant minority of patients may be comatose on admission or show focal neurologic abnormalities. Seizures are present in approximately 5% of patients. Nonspecific symptoms are more frequent in infants and the elderly. Overall, the mortality rate is 20–25%; pneumococcal meningitis has a higher mortality rate than meningococcal (30% vs. 7%).
    •  
      ABMs caused by invasive medical procedures or by trauma are associated with a different etiology of infecting organisms, including S. aureus and enteric gram-negative bacilli. Signs and symptoms depend on the infecting organism as well as the predisposing event; trauma-related symptoms may overlap with those of the subsequent infection and may delay diagnosis and intervention.
    •  
      The risk of meningitis is approximately 5–10% following compound skull fractures. The risk is increased when the wound is heavily contaminated with external material. Basilar skull fractures, which result in communication of the subarachnoid space with sinus cavities, are associated with a risk of meningitis up to 25%, with onset in the 2nd week after trauma. Persistent CSF leak may be associated with recurrent bacterial meningitis.
    •  
      Patients with aseptic meningitis most commonly present with photophobia, meningismus, headache, and fever. Patients with severe aseptic meningitis, however, may develop seizures and altered mental status and progress to significant neurologic dysfunction. Enteroviral meningitis shows seasonal variation, occurring primarily in the summer and fall. HSV meningitis, as opposed to encephalitis, is usually caused by HSV-2, and most patients report genital lesions with appearance in the week prior to CNS symptoms.

    Laboratory Findings

    •  
      In patients with signs and symptoms of meningitis, it is most critical to rule out bacterial meningitis on primary evaluation. Appropriate laboratory testing and cultures should be collected, followed by empirical antibiotic therapy as indicated. Because the subarachnoid space is the major reservoir of CSF, CSF is the specimen of choice for tests to diagnose meningitis caused by most pathogens. HSV should be considered in patients if encephalitis is present. It may be challenging to differentiate ABM from aseptic meningitis using nonspecific laboratory tests, like cell counts, because there may be some overlap within observed abnormalities in different patients and pathogens. See Chapter 3 for details of diagnostic testing.
    •  
      A complete history and physical examination is critical to assessing patient status and may suggest additional pathogens to consider for diagnostic testing.
    •  
      CT of the head should be obtained prior to lumbar puncture in patients with signs or symptoms of increased intracranial pressure.
    •  
      CSF studies: Opening pressure, culture and Gram stain, cell count and differential, glucose, and protein. Collect additional CSF for specific testing of agents suggested by clinical presentation, like HSV-2 PCR, or WNV IgM.
    •  
      Collect blood cultures and cultures of other potentially implicated sites, like trauma.
    •  
      Routine labs: CBC and differential, coagulation studies, electrolytes, and LFTs.
    •  
      Primary diagnostic testing may also include other laboratory diagnostic tests for a patient in whom clinical presentation, epidemiologic risk factors, or signs and symptoms suggest a high prior probability of a pathogen outside the normal etiology of meningitis.
    •  Typical Findings, Acute Bacterial Meningitis  
      •  
        CSF: Most patients have increased opening pressure at LP, increased WBC with >80% PMN, increased protein, and decreased glucose (CSF/serum ratio ≤0.4). Gram stain is positive in a majority of patients and morphology may provide important diagnostic information for specific pathogens. As opposed to other bacterial pathogens, Listeria typically shows increased mononuclear cells.
      •  
        Cultures: CSF and blood cultures, when collected before antibiotic therapy, are positive in a majority of patients with ABM.
      •  
        Routine laboratory tests: WBC is often elevated with a left shift. Platelets may be decreased. In severe infection, laboratory tests may indicate organ dysfunction, like DIC, hyponatremia, etc.
    •  Typical Findings, Aseptic Meningitis  
      •  
        CSF: Patients show mild pleocytosis. A PMN predominance may be seen early, developing to lymphocyte predominance as the disease progresses. Protein may be mildly elevated (<150 mg/dL) and glucose is normal (CSF/serum ratio ≥40%). The finding of markedly increased protein suggests WNV infection.
      •  
        Routine laboratory tests: Routine lab abnormalities are not used to make a specific diagnosis, but may indicate secondary organ dysfunction.
      •  Specific diagnosis: CSF PCR is used for enteroviral and HSV diagnosis. Studies of nasopharyngeal and feces may improve diagnosis of enteroviral meningitis. Other causes, including fungi, arboviruses, HIV, measles, mumps, LCMV, Lyme disease, syphilis, are diagnosed with appropriate serologic, NAAT, or culture methods.  
        •  
          Enteroviruses: The incidence of enteroviral meningitis peaks in late summer and early fall, but enteroviruses cause low-level, endemic disease year-round.
        •  
          HSV-2: A significant percentage of patients with primary genital herpes simplex infection also demonstrate signs and symptoms of aseptic meningitis. HSV-2 may also cause recurrent aseptic meningitis associated with flares of genital infection.
        •  
          HIV: A subset of patients with primary HIV infection will develop signs and symptoms of aseptic meningitis or meningoencephalitis, which is usually self-limited.
        •  
          Lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV): The virus is transmitted by the urine or feces of mice and other small rodents. There is an increased rate of infection during the winter months, presumably due to increased exposure. Aseptic meningitis caused by LCMV is unusual because CSF may show decreased glucose concentrations and WBC counts >1,000/mm3. Diagnosis is usually established serologically.
        •  
          Mumps virus: Aseptic meningitis is a fairly frequent complication of mumps infection, but the incidence has significantly decreased due to effective vaccination programs. However, localized outbreaks continue to occur. This diagnosis may be suspected in patients with concurrent or recent parotitis.

    Suggested Readings

    Brouwer MC, Tunkel AR, McKhann GM II, et al. Brain abscess. N Engl J Med. 2014;371:447–456.
    Pradilla G, Ardila GP, Hsu W, et al. Epidural abscesses of the CNS. Lancet Neurol. 2009;8:292–300.
    Tyler KL. Acute viral encephalitis. N Engl J Med. 2018;379:557–566.
    van de Beek D, de Gans J, Spanjaard L, et al. Clinical features and prognostic factors in adults with bacterial meningitis. N Engl J Med. 2004;351:1849–1859.
    van de Beek D, de Gans J, Tunkel AR, et al. Community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults. N Engl J Med. 2006;354:44–53.
    van de Beek D, Drake JM, Tunkel AR. Nosocomial bacterial meningitis. N Engl J Med. 2010; 362:146–154.

    PANDAS—Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal Infections

    * 

    Definition

    PANDAS is believed to result from an autoimmune disorder involving group A Streptococcus (GAS) that manifests with a variable combination of tics, obsessions, compulsions, and other symptoms that may be severe enough to be diagnosed as chronic tic disorder, OCD 

    Clinical Presentation

    •  The diagnostic criteria for PANDAS include sudden-onset OCD or severely restricted food intake and at least two of the following symptoms:  
      •  
        Anxiety
      •  
        Emotional lability or depression
      •  
        Irritability or aggression
      •  
        Behavioral regression
      •  
        Attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder-like symptoms that cause a deterioration in school performance
      •  
        Sensory or motor abnormalities
      •  
        Somatic symptoms like sleep disturbances and frequent urination
    •  
      PANDAS is a subset of pediatric acute-onset neuropsychiatric syndrome (PANS), which has the same symptoms but can be caused by infections like varicella or influenza.
    •  
      While there are no incidence data on PANS yet, estimates suggest that the disorder impacts about 1% of elementary school-aged children and likely is underdiagnosed. PANDAS primarily affects children younger than 12 years of age, perhaps because this group is less likely to have streptococcal antibodies.
    Treatment Recommendations
    •  
      Several novel therapies, including the use of intravenous immunoglobin (IVIG) and clinical trials including plasmapheresis have been used to treat acutely ill children, and antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent strep-triggered neuropsychiatric exacerbations. Subsequent work has revealed that the cross-reactive antibodies are unique to the PANDAS subgroup and have biologic activity in the CNS.
    •  
      It has long been established that in the treatment for rheumatic fever with Sydenham chorea, antibiotics may be recommended despite a negative strep throat culture. Prophylactic levels of antibiotics should be considered for children with severe symptoms of PANDAS and those recovering from immunotherapy or those with multiple GAS-associated neuropsychiatric exacerbations. Cognitive behavioral therapy can benefit those with mild impairments. A pediatrician may see a 7-year-old child who is normal at the beginning of the week and by the end of the week has severely debilitating OCD. These patients can have a significant recovery after IVIG. If symptoms persist, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, corticosteroids, IVIG, or therapeutic plasma exchange may be beneficial. IVIG and therapeutic plasma exchange, however, can be expensive and difficult to get approved by insurance.
     *Submitted by Michael Mitchell, MD.
     
     *Submitted by Edward Ginns.
